title: Elite Universities Lost Their Organizing Function — and Nobody Noticed

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
For decades, elite universities served a clear purpose: they were the gateway to becoming part of society's leadership class. Students endured the admissions gauntlet knowing that Harvard, Yale, or Stanford would connect them to the corridors of power — the CIA, Wall Street, the top law firms. That organizing function has completely disintegrated, leaving students adrift once they arrive on campus.

As Peter Thiel observes, high school still has an organizing function — its purpose is to get you into college. But once students reach elite institutions, there's no coherent next step. In the 1980s, Allan Bloom could provocatively address Harvard students as "my fellow elitists" because the pretense of egalitarianism masked a genuine elite-forming mission. Today, that speech would fall flat — not because the audience has become truly egalitarian, but because there is nothing elite about the institution anymore.

The institutions that elite universities once fed into have degraded in parallel. The CIA that once recruited the best and brightest from the Ivy League is no longer a prestige destination. The consulting firms, law partnerships, and banks that absorbed two-thirds of Stanford undergraduates in the late 1980s still exist, but the legal track — the "best" of the four — still produces mostly unhappy practitioners. The entire pipeline has corroded.

This creates a paradoxical opportunity. If the traditional prestige paths are hollowed out, the smartest young people should be flooding into technology — the one sector that has consistently generated meaningful progress over the past 25 to 30 years. Yet many elite campuses remain disconnected from this reality. Harvard students may not even register that Mark Zuckerberg built Facebook there. The institutions haven't caught up to 2005, let alone 2025.

The implication for builders and investors is stark: the talent pipeline from elite universities is broken not because the students lack ability, but because the institutions have no coherent vision for what excellence looks like anymore. New institutions like the University of Austin represent bets that the organizing function can be rebuilt from scratch — but only if they avoid replicating the very dysfunction they were created to escape.
